description: A comprehensive training program for building autonomous drone systems that inspect and monitor large industrial facilities: solar farms, wind turbines, oil refineries, chemical plants, power plants, and warehouses. The program covers all four engineering tracks—AI, MLOps, Data Engineering, and Edge AI—applied specifically to facility management and industrial inspection, enabling automated anomaly detection, predictive maintenance, and integration with enterprise asset management systems.
learning_objectives: Understand the types of industrial facilities and their critical inspection needs
Build and train AI models for detecting anomalies: thermal hotspots, corrosion, leaks, structural defects, and equipment malfunctions
Create geospatial and time‑series data pipelines for tracking asset health over time
Deploy optimized models on edge hardware for real‑time anomaly detection during flights
Implement automated reporting and integration with CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management Systems)
Design safe inspection flight paths in hazardous environments (GPS‑denied, confined spaces)
Comply with industrial safety regulations (OSHA, ATEX for explosive atmospheres)

modules.title: Module 1: Facility Management and Industrial Inspection Fundamentals

lessons.title: Types of Facilities and Inspection Needs
lessons.content: Study key industrial assets: solar farms (PV panels, inverters), wind turbines (blades, nacelles, towers), oil refineries (pipes, tanks, flare stacks), chemical plants (pressure vessels, piping, storage), power plants (cooling towers, turbines, transmission lines), warehouses (roofs, HVAC, structural integrity). Understand common defects: thermal anomalies (hot spots indicating electrical faults or insulation failure), corrosion, leaks (gas, liquid), structural cracks, missing components, vegetation encroachment, and equipment degradation. Learn inspection frequencies: continuous monitoring for critical assets, periodic (monthly, quarterly) for others, and post‑incident inspections.

lessons.title: Operational Scenarios and Requirements
lessons.content: Analyze real‑world scenarios: solar farm thermal inspection (identifying faulty cells), wind turbine blade inspection (cracks, erosion, lightning damage), refinery flare stack monitoring, chemical plant pipe leak detection (using optical gas imaging), warehouse roof inspection (ponding water, membrane tears). Define key requirements: specialized sensors (thermal, optical gas imaging, high‑zoom RGB), flight autonomy in GPS‑denied or confined spaces, compliance with hazardous area classifications (ATEX, Class I Div 1), real‑time anomaly alerting to maintenance teams, and integration with CMMS (e.g., IBM Maximo, SAP).

modules.title: Module 2: AI Engineer – Anomaly Detection and Predictive Maintenance

lessons.title: Thermal Anomaly Detection on Solar Panels and Electrical Equipment
lessons.content: Train a model to detect thermal anomalies in solar panels (hot cells, string failures) and electrical substations (overheating connections). Use a dataset of thermal images with labeled anomalies. Consider using a combination of object detection (to locate individual panels) and classification (normal vs. hot). For solar panels, compute temperature deviation relative to neighboring panels. For electrical equipment, use absolute temperature thresholds and rate of change. Output a heatmap of anomalies with severity.
lessons.code_example:
import torch
from ultralytics import YOLO
# Detect panel locations first
panel_detector = YOLO('solar_panel_detector.pt')
# Then for each panel, extract thermal values and classify anomaly

lessons.title: Wind Turbine Blade Defect Detection
lessons.content: Train a segmentation or detection model to identify cracks, erosion, lightning strikes, and ice accretion on wind turbine blades. Use high‑resolution RGB and optionally thermal imagery. Because blades are large and often viewed at oblique angles, use a model that can handle wide‑aspect‑ratio objects. Implement a blade tracking module to follow the blade in video and stitch multiple views for complete coverage.
lessons.code_example:
model = torch.hub.load('facebookresearch/detectron2', 'mask_rcnn_R_50_FPN_3x', pretrained=True)
# Fine‑tune on wind turbine blade defect dataset

lessons.title: Gas Leak Detection with Optical Gas Imaging (OGI)
lessons.content: Train a model to detect gas leaks (e.g., methane, hydrocarbons) from OGI camera footage (e.g., FLIR GF series). Use video sequences where leaks appear as plumes. Use a 3D CNN or a frame‑differencing approach to detect movement in specific spectral bands. Output bounding boxes or segmentation of the leak plume and estimate relative size. Integrate with gas concentration sensors if available.

lessons.title: Predictive Maintenance Models
lessons.content: Use time‑series data from multiple inspections to predict remaining useful life (RUL) of components. Build a regression model (e.g., LSTM, Transformer) that takes defect progression over time and outputs a probability of failure within the next X months. This enables condition‑based maintenance scheduling.

modules.title: Module 3: Data Engineer – Asset Health Database

lessons.title: Asset Hierarchy and Time‑Series Data with TimescaleDB
lessons.content: Create a database to store asset information (hierarchical: site → system → component → defect instance). Use PostgreSQL with TimescaleDB extension for efficient time‑series storage of inspection results. Each inspection record includes asset ID, timestamp, anomaly type, severity, image references, and recommended action.

lessons.title: Integration with CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management Systems)
lessons.content: Build APIs to push inspection results to enterprise systems like IBM Maximo, SAP, or open‑source alternatives. Define a standard data model for work orders: asset, defect description, priority, suggested action. Automate work order creation when critical anomalies are detected (e.g., high‑severity hot spot).
lessons.code_example:
import requests
work_order = {
    'asset_id': 'PV-1234',
    'description': 'Hot cell detected, temp 85°C, panel row 3 col 5',
    'priority': 'high',
    'action': 'inspect and replace'
}
requests.post('https://cmms-api/workorders', json=work_order)

lessons.title: 3D Digital Twins for Facilities
lessons.content: Use photogrammetry and LiDAR to create 3D digital twins of facilities. Store these as textured meshes or point clouds. Map inspection results onto the digital twin, allowing engineers to visualize defects in context. Update the digital twin after significant changes. Use open standards like 3D Tiles or GLTF for web delivery.
lessons.code_example: OpenDroneMap for orthophoto; CloudCompare for point cloud processing

modules.title: Module 4: Edge AI Engineer – Real‑Time Anomaly Detection on Drone

lessons.title: Hardware Configuration for Industrial Inspection
lessons.content: Select sensors based on facility type: high‑resolution RGB (20+ MP), thermal camera (e.g., FLIR Duo Pro R), optionally optical gas imaging camera (e.g., FLIR GF77), LiDAR for 3D mapping. Use a drone with RTK GPS and obstacle avoidance sensors (stereo cameras, LiDAR). For confined spaces, consider a collision‑tolerant drone (e.g., with cage). Connect to Jetson Orin Nano for on‑board AI processing. For hazardous areas, ensure equipment is rated for explosive atmospheres (ATEX certification).

lessons.title: DeepStream Pipeline for Multi‑Sensor Fusion
lessons.content: Build a DeepStream pipeline that ingests both RGB and thermal streams, performs object detection (e.g., solar panels, wind turbine blades), and runs anomaly classification on each detected component. Fuse the results: RGB for structural defects, thermal for temperature anomalies. Output an annotated video with overlays and transmit metadata (anomalies with coordinates) to ground station.

lessons.title: ROS 2 Integration for Autonomous Inspection and Reporting
lessons.content: Create a ROS 2 node that navigates the facility based on pre‑planned waypoints or uses visual SLAM for GPS‑denied environments (e.g., inside a warehouse or under solar panels). While flying, run anomaly detection and publish alerts to `/anomalies`. Another node logs these anomalies to a database. At the end of flight, generate a summary report with images and recommendations.

modules.title: Module 5: ML Platform Engineer – Continuous Improvement and Fleet Management

lessons.title: Active Learning for Rare Anomalies
lessons.content: Industrial facilities may have rare but critical anomalies (e.g., gas leaks, turbine blade cracks). Use active learning to prioritize images where the model is uncertain or where maintenance teams have confirmed false negatives. Feed these back to retrain the model, improving recall for critical defects.
lessons.code_example:
uncertainty_scores = model.predict_uncertainty(batch)
uncertain_samples = [sample for sample, score in zip(batch, uncertainty_scores) if score > threshold]

lessons.title: Kubeflow Pipelines for Model Retraining
lessons.content: Set up a Kubeflow pipeline that retrains anomaly detection models weekly or after each new batch of labeled data. Include steps: data validation, augmentation, training, evaluation, model registry (MLflow), and deployment to drone fleet via OTA. Version both models and datasets.
lessons.code_example:
from kfp import dsl
@dsl.pipeline
def industrial_model_retraining():
    data_prep = dsl.ContainerOp(...)
    train = dsl.ContainerOp(...).after(data_prep)
    evaluate = dsl.ContainerOp(...).after(train)
    deploy = dsl.ContainerOp(...).after(evaluate)

lessons.title: Fleet‑Wide Monitoring and Drift Detection
lessons.content: Monitor model performance across a fleet of drones inspecting multiple facilities. Use Evidently AI to compare data distributions (e.g., thermal histogram) across facilities and time. If drift is detected (new camera model, seasonal temperature changes), trigger retraining with facility‑specific data.

lessons.title: Hardware‑in‑the‑Loop (HIL) with Industrial Environment
lessons.content: Use NVIDIA Isaac Sim or a specialized industrial simulator (e.g., Unity with industrial assets) to create realistic facilities: solar farms, wind turbines, refinery structures. Simulate defects (hot spots, cracks, gas plumes). Connect the real Pixhawk and Jetson. Test autonomous inspection flights, anomaly detection, and reporting. Validate safety procedures in hazardous scenarios.
lessons.code_example: Isaac Sim with ROS2 bridge; drone flies predefined grid; Jetson runs models on simulated camera feeds

lessons.title: Latency Profiling for Real‑Time Alerts
lessons.content: Profile the detection‑to‑alert latency using Nsight Systems. For critical anomalies (e.g., gas leak, overheating), latency should be < 1 second to enable immediate pilot intervention. Optimize by using TensorRT, reducing model complexity, and parallelizing inference streams.

lessons.title: Industrial Safety Regulations
lessons.content: Study OSHA (US) and equivalent standards for drone operations in industrial environments: confined space entry, working at heights, hazardous atmospheres. For facilities with explosive environments, drones must be ATEX‑certified. Develop safety protocols: keep safe distance from high‑voltage equipment, avoid interfering with operations, use two‑person crew (pilot and observer).

lessons.title: Data Privacy and Security
lessons.content: Industrial inspection data may contain proprietary layouts or trade secrets. Encrypt data in transit and at rest. Limit access to authorized personnel. Use secure communication (e.g., encrypted Wi‑Fi or cellular). Implement audit trails for all flights and data access.

lessons.title: Environmental and Ethical Considerations
lessons.content: Consider the environmental impact of frequent drone flights (noise, emissions). Optimize flight plans to minimize unnecessary flights. Ensure that inspections do not disrupt facility operations (e.g., schedule during maintenance windows). For oil and gas, prioritize leak detection to prevent environmental harm.

capstone_project.name: Autonomous Solar Farm Inspection Drone
capstone_project.description: Build and test a drone system that autonomously inspects a solar farm (or simulated layout). The drone must fly a pre‑planned grid, capture RGB and thermal images, detect panels, identify hot cells, and generate a maintenance report with panel coordinates and thermal images. Achieve >95% recall for hot cells (temperature >10°C above ambient) and <5% false positives.
deliverables: Trained thermal anomaly detection model (quantized, <100 MB)
ROS 2 nodes for flight planning, anomaly detection, and reporting
HIL simulation report with performance metrics
Safety plan for operating near electrical equipment

resources.name: FLIR Thermal Dataset for Solar Panels
OSHA Safety Standards for Drone Operations
